methodology_long: Rankings come from a shadow roto model. Each week every team is scored across the fourteen active categories (R, HR, RBI, SB, AVG, OBP, SLG, W, K, ERA, WHIP, K/BB, QS, SV+H) on an estimate of true strength — not the head-to-head result, which depends on schedule luck. A team gets 12 points for leading a category down to 1 for last; the Power Score is the sum (max 168), and that ordering is the rank. Now that the season is past its midpoint, the preseason projection has been DROPPED entirely — every team is judged only on what it has actually done on the field. True strength is a weighted average of the thirteen completed weeks using exponential decay (λ=0.85), so the most recent week carries the most weight (~17%) and the four most recent weeks make up roughly 54% of the estimate. Rate categories are built from weighted components (total earned runs over total innings, total hits over total at-bats), not by averaging weekly rates. The strength/weakness badges reflect each team's full-season category ranks; the ordering reflects recent form.

rankings.blurb: Still the model's #1 at 142, and the margin over the field is built on the simple fact that there's no place to attack them. Even with the preseason prior now gone and the rankings judged purely on what's happened on the field, CLAUDE grades top-5 in eleven of the fourteen categories. They're the league's only triple-digit base-stealer (100 SB, a clear #1), lead the league in strikeouts (959, a 28-K cushion over Caminero), and are tied for the win lead (61, with Duran). The bats are 2nd in homers (143) and slugging (.450) and top-4 in average and OBP, while the staff is 2nd in both ERA (3.39) and WHIP (1.17). The three categories the badges flag as 'weak' — saves+holds, quality starts, K/BB — are all still 4th or 5th; there is no category where this team is genuinely bad. The recent trend is actually a power spike: 59 homers over the last four weeks (14.8/wk, up from 9.3/wk across the first nine) has added thump to what was already a speed-and-pitching club. Week 13 was a pitching clinic in a 9-4-3 win over Burns — 73.1 innings, 78 K, 2.45 ERA, 1.12 WHIP, 8 quality starts — even with the bats asleep (.206). The 8-2-3 matchup record and 112-60-36 category record are both the best in the league. The throne isn't in any obvious danger.

Up to #2 and closing on CLAUDE, and the climb is real rather than a one-week mirage — the rank line reads 4→4→3→2 over the last month. Spoons has been the best run-prevention staff in the league all year: #1 in ERA (3.27), #1 in WHIP (1.07), #1 in quality starts (66), and 2nd in both strikeouts and K/BB. What changed is the offense. Over the first nine weeks they averaged 33 runs and 7.9 homers a week; across the last four they've erupted to 42.5 runs and 15.2 homers a week, slugging .509 in that span. That power surge is exactly why they jumped Valdez. Week 13 was the single most dominant performance of the CFBL season — a 13-1-2 obliteration of Duran built on 50 runs, 19 homers, a .298/.371/.580 slash, and a 3.08 ERA with 79 K. The one structural hole is saves+holds, where they sit 11th (34) and look content to punt it. Their 113-62-33 category record has quietly become the best in the league, a hair ahead of CLAUDE's 112 — Spoons has technically won more individual categories than anyone this season. If the bats hold anywhere near this level, the #1 spot becomes a live question.

Holds at #3, and this is the team where dropping the preseason prior matters most. Last week's model had Valdez leading the league in runs AND homers — but those were stale projections of the old Universal Basic Civale roster, not what's actually happened, and the corrected picture is more honest. Valdez genuinely leads the league in RBI (456) and saves+holds (76, one clear of ShawSwank), and tops the league in K/BB (3.39, just ahead of Spoons). They're 2nd in runs (485, behind Burns' 512) and 2nd in OBP (.353, a whisker behind Burns' .357) — excellent, but not the runaway #1s the prior implied, and 4th (not 1st) in homers. The real story is the pitching turnaround from the May megatrade: through nine weeks the staff was a 4.53-ERA, 1.27-WHIP liability throwing just 37 innings a week, but over the last four it's a 3.56 ERA, 1.11 WHIP on 68 innings a week as Yamamoto, Valdez and Eovaldi have settled in. The lingering weakness is volume — dead-last in wins (33), strikeouts (611) and quality starts (32) because of those thin early innings. Week 13 was a clean 11-4-1 win over Balls (12 HR, 6 QS, 6 SV+H, 3.14 ERA). The rank slipped a spot only because Spoons' offense caught fire; Valdez itself hasn't cooled.

Steady at #4, a high-floor team with a clear identity: get on base, score runs, and roll out broad-but-unspectacular pitching. Burns leads the entire league in runs (512) and OBP (.357), and ranks top-4 in average (3rd), wins (tied-3rd), strikeouts (4th), quality starts (3rd), saves+holds (3rd), ERA (3rd), WHIP and K/BB — a remarkably wide base of competence with no glaring hole. The ceiling is capped by power: 11th in RBI (375), 8th in homers (112), 7th in slugging (.427). They win the on-base and rate battles every week but get out-muscled in the counting-power categories, which is why a 7-4-2 team grades 4th rather than 2nd. The profile has been stable all year (rank line 2→2→4→4) and slipped only because Spoons and Valdez surged past on the strength of their bats. Recent form underlines the trade-off: 163 runs over the last four weeks (still elite) on just 34 homers and 120 RBI in that span. The Week 13 loss to CLAUDE (4-9-3) was simply running into a 2.45-ERA buzzsaw. Burns is the best of the 'very good, not elite' tier and would need a power infusion to crack the top three.

Caminero sits 5th despite a matchup record (8-4-1, tied for 2nd-most wins) that argues for much higher. That gap between results and underlying strength has been the model's read all season, and it's been widening — the rank line is 3→3→5→5, a slow fade. The counting-stat strengths are real: 2nd in strikeouts (931), 3rd in homers (141) and wins (60), 4th in RBI (432) and slugging (.438). But the rate pitching drags the profile down: 8th in K/BB (2.67), 6th in WHIP, and the direction is wrong — staff ERA has climbed from 3.81 over the first nine weeks to 4.32 over the last four. The offense has also cooled, from 37.4 runs a week early to 34.0 recently. Week 13 was the upset of the week in the wrong direction: a 4-10-2 loss to lowly Camp, who picked that week to catch fire at the plate. The record says contender; the categories say a team living a bit above its means and vulnerable if the pitching rates don't stabilize. The 8 wins are banked, but the model expects regression unless the staff tightens up.

Up one to #6 on the back of the most fearsome offense in the league, and held down by the worst rate pitching among the contenders. PO' BOYD leads the CFBL in home runs (149) and slugging (.468) and ranks 2nd in average (.264) and 3rd in RBI (445) — when they connect, nobody hits harder. The problem is the mound: dead-last in K/BB (2.37), 10th in ERA (4.25), and the recent trend is alarming — staff ERA has ballooned from 3.71 over the first nine weeks to 5.47 over the last four. They essentially try to out-slug their own pitching every week, which produces wild scorelines and a middling 4-6-3 record. Week 13 was the template: an 18-homer, .511-slugging explosion that beat Polar 10-4-2 in spite of a 5.74 ERA. Over the last four weeks the bats have climbed another gear (40.8 runs a week, up from 32.6 early), so the offensive ceiling keeps rising even as the staff sinks. They're also slow (10th in steals), which costs a near-automatic category. If they ever drag the pitching up to merely below-average, this is a top-5 team; until then it's a high-variance, boom-or-bust outfit that can beat or lose to anyone.

Y&Y is the league's best pure-hitting team that can't pitch, and the model has them 7th. They lead the CFBL in batting average (.265) and rank top-4 in runs (4th, 477), slugging (3rd, .446) and OBP (4th) — a genuinely elite offensive profile. But the staff is the worst among the non-cellar teams: 11th in quality starts (33), 10th in strikeouts (749, a volume problem — they throw the fewest innings of any contender), and bottom-tier in WHIP on the season. The one encouraging sign is that the pitching has quietly improved — ERA down from 4.39 over the first nine weeks to 3.75 over the last four, WHIP from 1.35 to 1.27 — which is why the rank ticked back up from 9th to 7th. The 5-7-1 record is the signature of a team that wins three or four hitting categories every week and loses most of the pitching ones. Week 13 was exactly that: a 5-10-1 loss to ShawSwank despite a respectable offensive line. Until the quality-start and strikeout volume arrives, the ceiling stays in the 5-7 range no matter how good the bats are.

ShawSwank is the model's biggest 'record beats the profile' story after Caminero: an 8-5 matchup record (tied for 2nd in wins) sitting on a #8 underlying ranking. The formula is a specialist's formula — 2nd in saves+holds (75), tied-3rd in wins (60), 4th in steals (76), and top-6 in quality starts and strikeouts. They punt the power categories hard: 11th in average (.237), 11th in homers (109), 11th in slugging (.407), 10th in RBI (378). Essentially they concede the four slug-and-average categories every week and try to sweep saves, steals, wins and quality starts — a roster engineered to win 9-7-type matchups, and the record says it's working. The rank trajectory (11→8→9→8) shows a team that climbed out of the bottom tier in late spring and has held a steady middle-eight spot since. Week 13 was a clean 10-5-1 win over Y&Y (7 saves+holds, a 1.10 WHIP). The strategy has a hard ceiling — you cannot finish top-5 batting .237 — but it's a smart, internally consistent build that's turned punching-specialist categories into real standings points.

rankings.team_name: Camp of the New Orleans Saints
rankings.blurb: Camp climbs a spot to #9 and is quietly trending up after a midseason dip (rank line 8→10→10→9). The profile is balanced-but-modest: 4th in RBI (432), 5th in strikeouts (870) and K/BB (2.79), 6th in saves+holds, with a recent surge in pitcher wins (21 over the last four weeks, among the most in that span). The holes are on the rate and power side — 10th in average (.243), homers (109) and slugging, plus bottom-third WHIP — so they grind out counting stats rather than win rate categories. The headline event was Week 13: Camp pulled the upset of the week, beating 8-win Caminero 10-4-2 on their best offensive game of the season (53 RBI — the most by any team that week — 12 homers, and a .308 average). One hot week aside, the season-long bats are still bottom-three in average and slugging, so it shouldn't be mistaken for a breakout. The 5-7-1 record is an honest reflection of a team hovering just below the playoff line, with enough pitching volume to stay relevant and not enough offense to climb.

Balls takes the biggest fall on the board, dropping four spots to #10 — and unlike most of this week's movement, this is a genuine decline, not a methodology artifact. The rank line tells it: 9→7→6→10, a steady climb undone by an ugly Week 13 (a 4-11-1 loss to Valdez with 22 runs, 7 homers, 3 wins and zero saves+holds). The underlying team still owns one elite trait — 2nd in quality starts (60) — plus solid rate pitching (5th in ERA and WHIP, 7th in K/BB). But two categories are quietly killing them every week: dead-last in saves+holds (23, with multiple recent zero-save weeks as the bullpen has been stripped for starters) and 11th in steals (43). That's two near-automatic losses before a matchup even begins. The offense is middling across the board (6th in average, 8th in runs, RBI and slugging) with no standout. The 3-7-3 record is the worst among the non-bottom-two teams, and after the Week 13 collapse it looks like an accurate read on where this roster now sits rather than bad luck.

Duran climbs out of the cellar to #11 — but only because Polar's profile graded out even worse under the prior-free model; Duran itself has been stuck at 11th for a month (10→11→11→11). There is a real strength here that keeps them off the very bottom: pitching volume and speed. They're tied for the league lead in wins (61, with CLAUDE), 2nd in steals (95), and top-5 in quality starts (55). The offense, though, is the worst in the league: dead-last in homers (95), average (.234), OBP (.302) and slugging (.372). When you can't slug and you survive on speed and starter volume, you're punting four or five categories every single week. Week 13 was rock bottom — a 1-13-2 demolition by Spoons in which Duran hit .196 with 5 homers and zero steals, the worst single-week line by any team this season. The 4-8-1 record is the product of a roster with a clear pitching-and-speed identity and no answer whatsoever in the power categories. The wins and steals will keep them from ever being a true zero, but the offense caps this firmly in the bottom tier.

Polar lands at #12 under the prior-free model, swapping spots with Duran. The 2-11 record is the league's worst and the rank line has been a flat 12→12→12→12, but the profile isn't completely barren: Polar is 4th in saves+holds (61) and top-6 in homers (128) and slugging (.436) — leftover power from before the roster was gutted in the May megatrade with Valdez. Everything else, though, lives at or near the bottom: last in runs (390), RBI (362), steals (42) and ERA (4.52), 11th in wins (40) and strikeouts (649). So even when the bats show up, the staff hands it right back. Week 13 was the pattern in miniature — 14 homers and a .498 slug, but a 5.47 ERA and only 3 wins in a 4-10-2 loss to PO' BOYD. The recent direction is down on the mound, too: staff ERA up from 4.25 over the first nine weeks to 5.19 over the last four. With only the power and saves categories to lean on and a rotation that can't hold a lead, Polar is firmly into the draft-pick-accumulation phase of the season.
